entities.description: The Latin universal chronicle underlying the English translations and adaptations.

rights.basis: Ranulf Higden died in 1364; the medieval literary work is out of copyright.

access.entityLabel: John Trevisa's Middle English Polychronicon translation and short continuation (abstract version)

entities.label: John Trevisa's Middle English Polychronicon translation and short continuation (abstract version)
notes: Schema compromise: catalog_description represents an abstract textual version because the schema has no abstract-version entity type.
The version includes the short continuation counted with the Trevisa family, not as another work.

rights.basis: The translation was completed in 1387 and Trevisa died in 1402.
rights.component: medieval Middle English translation and continuation text

access.entityLabel: Anonymous second Middle English Polychronicon translation (abstract version)

dateStatements.label: probable translation date
dateStatements.notes: This intellectual-version inference is distinct from the sole manuscript witness's catalog date of 1450-1474.

entities.description: An independent complete seven-book translation, inferred by the Rolls editors to date probably c. 1432-1460 and surviving only in Harley MS 2261.
entities.label: Anonymous second Middle English Polychronicon translation (abstract version)
notes: Schema compromise: catalog_description represents an abstract textual version because the schema has no abstract-version entity type.

rights.basis: The anonymous translation is a fifteenth-century work.
rights.component: medieval anonymous Middle English translation text

access.entityLabel: Harley MS 1900 Book VI chapters 15-26 alternative (abstract version passage)
access.format: Rolls Series volume VII appendix pages [501]-538

entities.description: A zero-count Trevisa-version child printed in volume VII as an alternative passage on pages [501]-538.
entities.label: Harley MS 1900 Book VI chapters 15-26 alternative (abstract version passage)
notes: Schema compromise: catalog_description represents an abstract version passage because the schema has no abstract-version entity type.
The source begins at Harley MS 1900 fol. 248v (Rolls 248b); the exact manuscript endpoint remains unresolved.

rights.basis: The alternative is a passage in an early-fifteenth-century Trevisa witness.

access.entityLabel: William Caxton's 1482 Polycronicon adaptation and continuation (abstract version)
notes: ISTC links several holding records and digital resources, but the exact copy used by the Rolls editors is unknown.

entities.description: Caxton's printed modernization and adaptation of Trevisa with continuation from 1357 through 1460.
entities.label: William Caxton's 1482 Polycronicon adaptation and continuation (abstract version)
notes: Schema compromise: catalog_description represents an abstract print version because the schema has no abstract-version entity type.
The exact Caxton copy or copies used by the Rolls editors remain unidentified.

rights.basis: The work was printed in 1482 and Caxton died in 1491.

next_steps: Inspect the manuscript or obtain an authoritative folio-level description; no enquiry has been sent.
openQuestions.question: What exact folio in Harley MS 1900 ends the Book VI chapters 15-26 alternative printed on volume VII pages [501]-538?

next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Collections staff to identify scan-copy provenance and current image delivery; no enquiry has been sent.
openQuestions.question: Which physical copies supplied U-M's 5,176 page images, and what current direct image-delivery route, if any, is publicly usable?

next_steps: Review all Rolls Series introductions and collation sigla against surviving ISTC holdings; no holding institution has been contacted.
openQuestions.question: Which exact Caxton 1482 copy or copies did the Rolls Series editors use for the selected readings and continuation?

next_steps: Seek item-level production documentation from U-M; no enquiry has been sent.
openQuestions.question: How should the collection-level manual-keying/proofreading account be reconciled with this item's header statement that it was OCR-created without corrections or content editing?

reviewNotes: The schema has no abstract-work or abstract-version entity types. The Latin Polychronicon and the four English version-level nodes are represented as catalog_description entities solely as a schema-native compromise; their notes identify the abstraction explicitly.

Access and rights apply only to the named entity and component. Printed volume-expression rights, scan-carrier/provider terms, derivative-file rights, catalog-data licenses, manuscript text, and manuscript-image supply terms are not inherited across the graph.

The current U-M item exposes a viewer link, but an automated request to a sample image route returned HTTP 403. That does not prove direct public download or unavailability; the 5,176-image layer remains access status unknown and is not cached as a current image set.
Volume IX contains no target work text but remains in the complete nine-volume edition and is retained as target-absent boundary evidence.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact pins a U-M/CME encoding of selected Middle English material in the nine-volume Rolls Series edition: Trevisa's translation and short continuation, an independent anonymous translation, a Harley MS 1900 alternative, and selected Caxton adaptation/continuation text. The graph separates intellectual-version nodes, manuscript witnesses, one aggregate edition and nine printed volume expressions, nine IA/Google scan carriers, derivative OCR/scandata and metadata objects, the historical U-M page-image layer, the current Medictionary reformat, and supporting catalog or scholarly records.

catalogSummary.author: Ranulf Higden (Latin source compiler); John Trevisa, an anonymous second translator, and William Caxton (English versions and continuations)
catalogSummary.date: Trevisa translation completed 1387; anonymous translation probably c. 1432-1460; Caxton adaptation and continuation printed 1482; source edition published 1865-1886
catalogSummary.editor: Churchill Babington and Joseph Rawson Lumby
catalogSummary.title: Middle English translations and continuations of Ranulf Higden's Polychronicon (Rolls Series edited selection)

catalogingSubject.label: Middle English translations and continuations of Ranulf Higden's Polychronicon (Rolls Series edited selection)
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The intellectual and content unit selected in the U-M/CME encoding AHB1341.0001.001: John Trevisa's seven-book Middle English translation with its short continuation, an anonymous second seven-book Middle English translation, and William Caxton's adaptation/continuation complex. The alternative Book VI passage from Harley MS 1900 is a zero-count version child of the Trevisa family. The subject is not Higden's complete Latin Polychronicon, the complete nine-volume Rolls Series edition, any manuscript as a physical object, or either digital carrier.

assertion.notes: The intellectual version's inferred translation range is distinct from the sole witness's catalog date of 1450-1474.

extent.description: Three count-once intellectual-family items are represented by 17 target DIV1 streams and 613 target DIV2 chapters in the pinned XML. The manifest contains 20 content records: three count-bearing parents and 17 zero-count children. Here `sections` means the 17 target DIV1 stream axis, not the 20 manifest records. All 73 LG elements and 852 L elements are main-stream text rather than NOTE1 apparatus. Trevisa (624 verse lines, including one 470-line group) and the anonymous translation (211 verse lines, including sustained Book VII verse) are mixed; Caxton remains prose-only because its 17 verse lines are brief quotations.

formStatements.description: The cataloging subject combines prose chronicle with substantive verse in two of the three count-once families. Trevisa Book I contains a 470-line verse group within 624 Trevisa-family verse lines; the anonymous family contains 211 verse lines, including sustained verse in Book VII. Caxton's 17 verse lines are brief quotations and do not cross the substantive-mixed threshold, so that family remains prose-only.

notes: The 17 target DIV1 streams, 613 DIV2 chapters, three count-once intellectual items, and 20 manifest content records are distinct axes and must not be conflated.

All 20 content parts carry explicit item_count and all five metrics. Count-bearing parents carry one prose-only and two mixed-item classifications; zero-count structural children carry the 73 verse-group and 852 verse-line measurements without double counting.

The lineage schema has no abstract-work or abstract-version entity types. The Latin work and English version nodes are therefore represented explicitly as catalog_description entities, with notes identifying that schema compromise.

The repository source XML, current U-M encoding, current Medictionary reformat, printed volume expressions, digital facsimile carriers, carrier metadata, and physical witnesses are distinct layers. Latin and Modern English in headings, citations, parallel columns, or editorial apparatus are representation details rather than work-discovery language facets.

next_steps: Inspect the manuscript or obtain an authoritative folio-level description; no enquiry has been sent.
openQuestions.question: What exact folio in Harley MS 1900 ends the Book VI chapters 15-26 alternative printed on Rolls Series volume VII pages [501]-538?

next_steps: Ask U-M Digital Collections staff to identify scan-copy provenance and current image delivery; no enquiry has been sent.
openQuestions.question: Which physical copies supplied U-M's 5,176 page images, and what current direct image-delivery route, if any, is publicly usable?

next_steps: Review all Rolls Series editorial introductions and collation sigla against surviving ISTC holdings; no holding institution has been contacted.
openQuestions.question: Which exact Caxton 1482 copy or copies did the Rolls Series editors use for the selected readings and continuation?

next_steps: Seek item-level production documentation from U-M; no enquiry has been sent.
openQuestions.question: How should the collection-level manual-keying/proofreading account be reconciled with this item's header statement that it was OCR-created without corrections or content editing?

notes: This does not make Higden the author of the English translations or Caxton's continuation.

scope.description: Underlying Latin universal chronicle translated or adapted by the three English intellectual families

assertion.notes: Caxton modernized and adapted Trevisa and supplied the continuation through 1460 represented by the selected late-Book-VII and Book-VIII streams.

scope.description: OCR-derived U-M digital encoding represented by the pinned repository XML

summaries.value: An edited selection of the English textual tradition of Ranulf Higden's universal chronicle: John Trevisa's complete seven-book translation and short continuation, an independent anonymous seven-book translation, and William Caxton's modernized adaptation and continuation through 1460.

The target has 17 encoded DIV1 streams and 613 DIV2 chapter divisions: seven Trevisa books, seven anonymous-translation books, one Harley MS 1900 Book VI alternative passage, Caxton's selected late Book VII passage, and Caxton's Book VIII continuation. The manifest summarizes those axes in 20 content records: three count-bearing intellectual-family parents plus 17 zero-count structural/version children.

Count once at the intellectual-family level: Trevisa translation plus short continuation; anonymous second translation; Caxton adaptation/continuation complex. Seven-book divisions, Caxton's Book VII and Book VIII streams, 613 chapters, and the Harley MS 1900 alternative are structural or version records and do not increase the intellectual item count.

assertion.notes: A project-supplied title that names the cataloging boundary and avoids treating the three English intellectual families as one medieval work.

titles.value: Middle English translations and continuations of Ranulf Higden's Polychronicon (Rolls Series edited selection)

notes: The spelling 'maonachi' is preserved exactly from the U-M bibliographic title; the printed edition reads 'monachi'.
titles.value: Polychronicon Ranulphi Higden maonachi Cestrensis; together with the English translations of John Trevisa and of an unknown writer of the fifteenth century.

publication.embeddedTitle: POLYCHRONICON RANULPHI HIGDEN MONACHI CESTRENSIS; — TOGETHER WITH THE ENGLISH TRANSLATIONS OF JOHN TREVISA AND OF AN UNKNOWN WRITER OF THE FIFTEENTH CENTURY.

work.summary: An edited selection of the English textual tradition of Ranulf Higden's universal chronicle: John Trevisa's complete seven-book translation and short continuation, an independent anonymous seven-book translation, and William Caxton's modernized adaptation and continuation through 1460.
